pattern: With only a handful of elements the transformation graph is tiny, so a breadth-first search over reachable arrangements finds the fewest split-and-merge moves.
hints: Each operation lifts one contiguous block and drops it elsewhere; the array length never changes and stays a permutation of the original.
Treat every distinct arrangement as a node and every legal lift-and-drop as an edge, then the answer is a shortest path length.
Run BFS from nums1: from a state, for every subarray [L..R] and every insertion gap, build the next arrangement; the first time you reach nums2 gives the minimum count.

edge_inputs.description: all identical elements need zero moves

stress.description: random target permutations of a random base array of length up to 6
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randrange(2, 7)
    base = [rng.randrange(-5, 6) for _ in range(n)]
    target = base[:]
    rng.shuffle(target)
    return [base, target]
stress.note: small value range introduces duplicate elements
